description: GE Aerospace designs, manufactures, and services commercial and military jet engines—including through its 50/50 CFM International joint venture with Safran—operating the world's largest installed commercial engine base and a services backlog exceeding $170 billion.

overview.title: From Conglomerate to Pure-Play
paragraphs: GE Aerospace is the end state of one of the most consequential industrial deconglomerations in U.S. corporate history. Between 2023 and 2024, the remnants of General Electric shed GE HealthCare and GE Vernova, leaving behind a focused aviation propulsion company headquartered in Cincinnati, Ohio. The renamed entity went public as GE Aerospace in April 2024, trading under the original GE ticker that has graced the Dow Jones Industrial Average longer than any other company.

CEO Larry Culp, the first outsider to lead GE in its 130-year history, engineered the restructuring after arriving in 2018 to find a balance sheet carrying roughly $100 billion in industrial debt. The resulting pure-play is considerably easier to value than its predecessor: it makes engines, it services engines, and it collects fees on every hour those engines fly.

overview.title: Products and Programs
paragraphs: GE Aerospace's commercial franchise is anchored by two engine families. The CFM56—developed through CFM International, its 50/50 joint venture with France's Safran Aircraft Engines—has logged over 33,000 deliveries to more than 600 operators and remains the most widely flown commercial engine in history. Its successor, the LEAP, powers the Airbus A320neo, Boeing 737 MAX, and COMAC C919 families, with GE and Safran sharing production and the accumulated royalties of a world-class narrowbody duopoly. In widebody, the GE9X holds an exclusive position on the long-delayed Boeing 777X.

The defense segment is smaller but strategically significant. GE's F414 turbofan powers the F/A-18 Super Hornet and Sweden's Gripen, while the T700 turboshaft has been the backbone of U.S. Army rotary-wing aviation for decades. The company is competing with its adaptive-cycle XA100 engine for the F-35 Engine Improvement Program, a contract that would extend GE's presence deep into the next generation of tactical aviation.

overview.title: The Services Flywheel
paragraphs: Engine manufacturing is a prestige business with thin OEM margins; engine servicing is where the economics become compelling. GE Aerospace's aftermarket agreements—structured as long-term 'Flight Hours' and 'TrueChoice' contracts—convert the installed base of roughly 44,000 CFM56 and LEAP engines into a multi-decade annuity. Services account for the majority of revenue and carry meaningfully higher margins than new-engine sales, a dynamic that the $170 billion backlog makes highly visible.

The aftermarket advantage is durable because jet engine certification and qualification barriers are steep—an airline cannot simply switch MRO providers without regulatory exposure—and because GE holds proprietary data from millions of flight cycles. The Flight Pulse analytics platform turns that sensor data into predictive maintenance schedules, reducing costly unplanned removals and tightening the operator relationship further.

overview.title: Technology Roadmap
paragraphs: The most consequential long-range bet is CFM RISE (Revolutionary Innovation for Sustainable Engines), announced in 2021 with Safran. The program targets an open-fan architecture—unducted, counter-rotating blades with no conventional nacelle—designed to deliver more than 20 percent better fuel efficiency than the LEAP while remaining compatible with sustainable aviation fuel and, potentially, hydrogen. Ground tests began in 2024, with a flight demonstrator targeted for the mid-2020s and a possible entry-into-service in the mid-2030s. The gap between 'targeted' and 'certified' in commercial aviation is long enough to deserve skepticism, but the physics of open-fan efficiency are well understood.

GE Aerospace is also a genuine pioneer in advanced manufacturing: LEAP fuel nozzles were among the first FAA-certified additive manufactured parts in a production engine, and its ceramic matrix composite (CMC) hot-section components—produced at a dedicated facility in Asheville, North Carolina—allow higher turbine temperatures with lower weight than conventional nickel superalloys, yielding efficiency gains that competitors have struggled to replicate at scale.

overview.title: Competitive Landscape and Key Risks
paragraphs: GE Aerospace's primary narrowbody competitor, Pratt & Whitney, handed it an inadvertent gift in 2023 when a powder metal contamination issue in the GTF engine required the inspection and grounding of hundreds of A320neo-family aircraft. The episode reinforced operator preference for LEAP and accelerated MRO revenue at GE-aligned shops. In widebody, Rolls-Royce dominates the A350 with its Trent XWB; GE holds the 777 franchise exclusively and competes with its GEnx on the 787.

The principal risk is Boeing. GE Aerospace's LEAP-1B fortunes track directly with 737 MAX production rates, which have been persistently constrained by Boeing's manufacturing quality problems and FAA oversight since the 2018-2019 grounding. A sustained Boeing recovery would meaningfully accelerate LEAP deliveries and aftermarket ramp; a further deterioration would clip growth targets. The 777X program, now years behind its original 2020 entry-into-service schedule, represents a similar concentration risk for the GE9X.

sourceSuggestions.title: GE Aerospace Investor Relations
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Primary source for earnings releases, annual reports, segment financials, and management guidance on backlog and free cash flow.

sourceSuggestions.title: GE Aerospace Corporate Website
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Product portfolio, engine program overviews, sustainability commitments, and press releases on defense contracts and technology milestones.

sourceSuggestions.title: CFM International – LEAP and RISE Programs
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Joint venture site covering LEAP production data, RISE open-fan program updates, and CFM56 installed-base statistics central to GE's aftermarket thesis.

sourceSuggestions.title: SEC EDGAR – GE Aerospace 10-K and 10-Q Filings
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Authoritative segment revenue breakdowns, backlog disclosures, pension obligations, and risk factor language directly from management.

sourceSuggestions.title: CompaniesMarketCap – GE Aerospace Historical Market Cap
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Time-series market capitalization data useful for contextualizing GE's valuation trajectory through the conglomerate breakup.

sourceSuggestions.title: Aviation Week Network – GE Aerospace Coverage
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Industry trade publication with detailed LEAP production rate reporting, defense engine program updates, and CFM RISE technical analysis not found in investor materials.

sourceSuggestions.title: U.S. Department of Defense Contract Announcements
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Primary source for GE Aerospace defense engine awards, including F414, T700, and adaptive-cycle program contract values and program-of-record status.

sourceSuggestions.title: FAA Aircraft Engine Type Certificate Data Sheets
sourceSuggestions.whyItMatters: Regulatory baseline for GE engine certifications; useful for verifying thrust ratings, approved fuel specifications, and airworthiness basis for each program.
